entry.title: Partner‑Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
entry.content:
In 2025, SAP expanded the Joule ecosystem with Joule Studio, enabling partners to extend Joule through custom skills and autonomous agents. Earlier initiatives demonstrated how partners could build Joule skills to handle targeted workflows and augment enterprise processes, as showcased in the Hack2Build custom Joule skills edition . 

With the Agent Builder now generally available, (read the announcement blog here), SAP has taken a major step forward. Partners can use agentic AI to design custom AI agents capable of planning, reasoning, and executing multi‑step processes across SAP and non‑SAP systems, supported by capabilities such as MCP‑based integrations, multi‑agent orchestration, and business‑context grounding. 

Against this backdrop, the Hack2Build on Custom AI Agents invited partners to explore these capabilities hands‑on for this inaugural edition. 

 

Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition 

The Hack2Build Custom AI Agents Edition focused on a select group of early‑adopter partners who received hands‑on coaching from SAP experts to work with the newly released Agent Builder capability. SAP Product and solution architecture specialists supported partners in shaping their designs and aligning them with specific customer needs.

SAP Partners participated in Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

 Partners overcame early product‑maturity challenges and succeeded in building functional custom AI agents in less than seven days during the hack phase. The initiative culminated in a Live Demo Jam, where all teams presented their working prototypes to a panel of SAP judges.

This was the first Hack2Build where partners worked directly with agentic AI tooling, experimenting with autonomous reasoning, multi‑agent orchestration, system integration patterns, and MCP‑enabled workflows.

🏆Winners

The winning solutions stood out for addressing customer‑critical challenges while thoughtfully complementing SAP’s portfolio. With creative use of agentic AI, the teams demonstrated how autonomous agents can be embedded into real workflows—connecting systems, reasoning over business data, and driving outcomes that matter to customers.

🥇Winner — LTIMindtree: Treasury Auto Ops

Treasury Auto Ops applies agentic AI to transform treasury management with real‑time forecasting, FX exposure assessment, liquidity oversight, and automated hedging recommendations. The agent synthesizes market indicators, macroeconomic signals, and internal treasury data to reach high forecasting accuracy and proactively execute SAP Treasury actions with policy‑compliant governance.
Partners selected for Hack2Build custom AI Agents first edition- January 2026

🥈1st Runner‑Up — Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

Avvale's agent identifies early‑payment discount opportunities across supplier invoices and provides optimized payment recommendations. By balancing liquidity needs with discount potential, it improves working capital, strengthens supplier relationships, and delivers measurable ROI.
Avvale: Early Payment Assistant

 🥉 2nd Runner‑Up — Talan: TALK2BUY

TALK2BUY brings conversational procurement to SAP Cloud ERP. Users can speak or type purchase requisitions in natural language, while the agent enforces procurement policy, recommends compliant items, and sends Microsoft Teams reminders to ensure timely goods‑receipt updates.

Talan: TALK2BUY

 ⭐ Finalists: High‑Quality Solutions from All Participating Partners

  • All for One – Service preparation, escalation & ticket automation agent
  • Capgemini – Smart Vendor Agent to assess supplier data consistency 
  • cbs  – ARIA: Agentic Rate & Inforecord Automation agent
  • DXC Technology – AI Engineering Proposal Assistant (JAEPA)
  • KPMG – Inventory Reconciliation Agent
  • Neuman – Financial Performance Advisor
  • NTT Data – FraudCheck Agent
  • Sodales – Job Accommodation & Return‑to‑Work program generator

Special Thanks

A sincere thank you to all participating partners, judges Pavel Penaz, Jan Schneider, Stefan Koenig, Boris Andree, the SAP Build and Joule Studio product teams, and the wider SAP Partner Ecosystem Success units for their support throughout the hack phase.

What’s Next?

The Hack2Build initiative now moves into the BUILD phase, where all participating partners will work over the next 4–5 weeks to realize the full scope of their agentic solutions. Coached by SAP experts, this phase focuses on maturing designs, enriching integrations, and preparing customer‑ready outcomes based on real business requirements and partner domain expertise.

For partners  interested in joining future Hack2Build, registration for the next edition in March is already open.

entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP): Governing APIs via CPI Provider - Part I
entry.content:
DCRP: Taming Enterprise-Scale SAP Integration API Proxies

Executive Summary

Enterprise SAP Integration environments with many integrations are often affected by "proxy sprawl." Each new integration needs an additional dedicated API proxy, which causes a one-to-one relationship and leads to bottlenecks in the deployment process, inconsistency in the security architecture and unacceptable growth of technical debt.

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) is a governance approach that brings together several backend services as part of a single, domain-based API Proxy. Using a metadata-driven routing engine (JavaScript + KVM) will allow the DCRP to reduce the number of proxies required by up to 96%, while ensuring that all enterprise-wide architectural requirements are enforced.

  • Impact on the Deployment: 4 business domains can be managed in 8 minutes.
  • On-boarding: It takes 30 seconds to add new endpoints to the system through metadata update.
  • Governance: Centralized Security, Analytics and Traceability.
My Contribution - Ricardo Viana

Dynamic routing is a native feature in SAP APIM, AWS, and Kong. However, technology alone isn't enough. DCRP provides the governance framework necessary to scale this technology in enterprise environments, including standardized naming conventions, KVM structures, and deployment templates.

⏱️ Time Investment: 

  • ✅Executive summary: 90 seconds -
  • ✅Full technical guide:25 minutes -
  • ✅Implementation in your landscape: 2-4 hours for first PoC

"Patterns are the 'things' that repeat in a design; pattern languages are the way we capture them."
Christopher Alexander

 

  • DCRP: Taming Enterprise-Scale SAP Integration API Proxies
  • Executive Summary
  • Introduction
  • 1 - The "Sprawl" Problem - An Overview
  • 2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern) ?
  • 2.3 - DCRP Architecture Overview: The Power of Dynamic Routing
  • 2.4 - DCRP in Context: Pattern vs. Technology
  • 3 - The API Proxy Design Pattern provides:
  • 4 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy
  • 4.3 - API Proxy Details
  • 4.4 - Endpoint & Routing Configuration
  • 5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions
  • 5.1 - Key Benefits:
  • 6 - The Semantic Bridge: Public API to Technical Endpoints
  • 6.1 - Key Observations:
  • 6.2 – GET Request Examples: Maintaining Protocol Integrity
  • 7 - The Routing Engine: Understanding the API-Proxy Execution Flow
  • 7.3 - The DCRP Brain executes 4 policy stages:
  • 8 - The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic
  • 8.1 - Key-Value Map (KVM): The Memory
  • 8.2 Scaling Model: Vertical vs. Horizontal
  • Key Constraints by Layer:
  • 8.6 - The Template Philosophy: Scaling Strategies
  • Two Strategic Approaches:
  • 8.7 - The ROI of Domain Templates
  • Real-World Lesson Learned: The "Ghost KVM" 404
  • 8.8 - The Domain Template Contract: Efficiency Through Pre-Configuration
  • What’s Pre-Configured (Zero Risk of Forgetting):
  • What You Change (30 seconds):
  • 9 - Deployment Checklist (Domain Template Strategy)
  • 9.1 - Critical Pre-Requisite for DCRP Validation
  • Parallel Development Workflow
  • 9. The Brain: Dissecting the JavaScript Routing Engine
  • 9.4 - How the Routing Engine Works
  • Section 1: Context Extraction
  • Section 2: Flexible Delimiter Detection and KVM Parsing
  • 10 -API Contracts, Versioning & Openapi Management
  • 10.1 - Why DCRP Proxies Don't Need Versioning
  • 10.2 - OpenAPI Management: The Hybrid Approach
  • The Execution Flow:
  • 10.3 - KVM vs OpenAPI: Complementary, Not Redundant
  • 10.4 - Version Coexistence and Deprecation
  • 11 -Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence
  • 12. Final Roadmap: Preparing for Scale
  • Pre-Scaling Checklist:
  • Team Alignment: Ensure developers, architects, and platform owners understand the move from 1:1 proxies to domain-centric routing.
  • Conclusion: Discipline Over Chaos
  • Benefits of the DCRP
  • The Roadmap Ahead

Introduction

SAP’s official documentation explains how to enable dynamic routing, but it often leaves a gap regarding when, why, and how to govern it at scale. This article aims to bridge that gap by providing a complete framework—from architectural design to hands-on implementation.

Building on my previous post about Advanced Domain Routing, we will now deep-dive into applying the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) to a real-world scenario: Sales & Distribution (O2C).

The DCRP Value Proposition:
  • Consolidation: A single API Proxy manages multiple sources (Salesforce, Microsoft D365, Vendor CRM).
  • Governance: Full request traceability and standardized security without infrastructure sprawl.
  • Scalability: High-performance routing without the overhead of proxy-level versioning.
What to Expect:

This is Part I of a series dedicated to eliminating integration chaos:

  • Part I (This post): APIM Architecture and DCRP Implementation.
  • Part II: Solving "CPI Package Sprawl" at the orchestration layer.
  • Part III: End-to-end Testing and Monitoring strategy.

Let’s explore the architectural rationale and the step-by-step implementation to apply DCRP in your landscape.

Figure 1 - DCRP - API Proxy Model detailed exploration

1 - The "Sprawl" Problem - An Overview

In many enterprise landscapes, it is common to find a 1:1 relationship between services and API Proxies. This leads to what we identify as "Proxy Sprawl": an environment where proxies are created for every individual technical service, regardless of their business domain.

Without a central governance authority or standardized naming conventions, these environments quickly become un-governed and unsustainable. As the integration volume grows, the complexity of managing, securing, and monitoring hundreds of individual proxies creates a significant operational bottleneck.

Figure 2 - The Sprawl Problem 

This pattern is not theoretical; DCRP was developed as a direct response to these real-world governance gaps. Following extensive architectural validation, this methodology was formalized to provide a repeatable solution for the SAP Integration Community.

Note: The following diagram is a conceptual illustration. In typical enterprise environments, the scale and complexity of these interconnections are significantly higher, making the need for structured governance even more critical.

2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern) ?

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) is an architectural design pattern that consolidates multiple backend services into a single, business-domain-oriented API Proxy. Residing at the Gateway Layer, DCRP governs how backend services are exposed and routed without necessitating changes to their underlying protocols (REST or SOAP).

Instead of organizing APIs into technical silos, DCRP aligns them with the organization's business domains. This approach significantly reduces API fragmentation and maintenance overhead while remaining fully compatible with SAP’s API Product offerings.

Figure 3 - The threr pillars of DCPR

2.2 - DCRP Decision Framework

Architecture is about choosing the right tool for the specific problem. DCRP is a high-scale pattern, and its implementation should be justified by the complexity of the landscape.

Use DCRP When:
  • Domain Density: You have 10+ APIs within the same business domain.
  • High Volatility: Integration changes occur frequently (more than once per quarter).
  • Centralized Governance: There is a critical need for unified security and policy enforcement.
  • Velocity: Rapid deployment speed is a key business requirement.
Do NOT Use When:
❌   Unrelated Services: You are dealing with mixed, unrelated domains that share no business logic.
❌   Low Density: There are only 1–2 services per domain.
❌   Minimal Footprint: Small environments with fewer than 10 APIs in total.
❌ Process Immaturity: The technical teams lack the governance maturity required to maintain a metadata-driven architecture.

Figure 4 - Decision when and not

2.3 - DCRP Architecture Overview: The Power of Dynamic Routing

The diagram below illustrates how the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) transforms the integration flow. Instead of a fragmented landscape of individual proxies, we consolidate connectivity through a unified, intelligent gateway layer.

The Technical Workflow:
  • Unified Entry Point: Multiple consumer systems—such as Salesforce, Dynamics 365, and Vendor CRMs—connect through a single Sales Domain APIM Proxy.
  • The DCRP Engine: Within the SAP API Management Gateway, the request is processed by the "Brain" of the pattern, which performs a KVM Lookup and executes JavaScript logic to resolve the target backend dynamically.
  • Secure Orchestration: The request is routed via a secure tunnel to the appropriate Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) iFlow.
  • Backend Flexibility: CPI handles the final orchestration with target systems (e.g., S/4HANA, Legacy Systems, or SAP ECC) using protocols like ODATA, SOAP, or HTTPS.
  • Governance at Scale: This model eliminates the need for separate proxies per integration, allowing for centralized monitoring, auditing, and security enforcement through the Analytics Collector and a global Governance Layer.

Figure 5 - DCRP Architecture Overview end-to-end: Three-CRM Integration Scenario

2.4 - DCRP in Context: Pattern vs. Technology

DCRP is a governance methodology designed, developed, and empirically validated through extensive testing across multiple SAP BTP integration landscapes. It synthesizes foundational architectural patterns into a disciplined, repeatable framework.

2.4.1 - The Technical Foundation: KVM + JavaScript

The core of DCRP relies on the strategic combination of two native SAP API Management capabilities, effectively creating a decoupled routing engine:

  • KeyValueMap (KVM) | The "Memory": Persistent metadata storage that contains the routing mappings in a httppath:iflowname format. By storing business logic as data rather than hardcoded endpoints, the KVM allows for near-instant configuration changes without redeploying code.
  • JavaScript Policy | The "Brain": Programmable logic that executes decisions at runtime. It queries the KVM, validates request paths, dynamically constructs target URLs, and injects essential governance headers such as X-SenderId and X-CorrelationId. It also provides structured error handling to prevent "silent failures".

The Innovation: While these capabilities exist natively in SAP APIM and other market platforms, DCRP provides the disciplined methodology required to govern their combination at enterprise scale. This includes standardized naming conventions, a clear template philosophy, and a unified analytics framework for end-to-end monitoring.

Figure 6 - KVM + Java Script Policy

2.5 - DCRP Across API Management Platforms

While this methodology is detailed using SAP APIM, the core principles of dynamic routing are platform-agnostic. The implementation logic remains consistent across the market, whether you are using AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management, or Kong.

2.6 - Proxy Sprawl vs. DCRP: Key Differentiators

To summarize the shift in mindset, let’s compare the traditional approach with the Domain-Centric methodology:

MetricProxy Sprawl (Traditional)DCRP (Domain-Centric)
Deployment Model1 proxy per integration (1:1)1 proxy per business domain
Scaling MethodDeploy new proxy (avg. 15 min)Update KVM metadata (~30 seconds)
GovernanceFragmented & inconsistent policiesCentralized & standardized templates
AnalyticsScattered monitoringUnified domain-level insights
2.6 - DCRP Across API Management Platforms

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern is an architectural methodology, not just a tool-specific feature. While we use SAP APIM for this guide, the core logic—using a metadata "memory" to resolve targets dynamically—is portable across all major enterprise gateways.

Portability Comparison

The technical implementation varies by platform, but the DCRP framework remains the same:

  • SAP APIM / Apigee: Low Complexity. Native support for KVM and JavaScript policies.
  • AWS / Azure / Kong / MuleSoft: Medium Complexity. Requires custom integration (e.g., Lambda, DynamoDB, or Plugin development).

Figure 7- SAP APIM ( DCRP ) vs Market

2.7 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP

The shift from a traditional 1:1 model to a Domain-Centric approach fundamentally changes how an integration landscape scales. The following comparison highlights the efficiency gains of the DCRP methodology:

FeatureProxy Sprawl (Traditional)DCRP (Domain-Centric)
Deployment Model1 proxy per integration (e.g., 100 integrations = 100 proxies)1 proxy per business domain (e.g., 100 integrations ≈ 10 domain proxies)
Scaling MethodDeploy a new proxy for every integration (~15 min each)Update KVM metadata for new integrations (~30 seconds, no redeployment)
GovernanceInconsistent naming and scattered monitoringMandatory naming conventions and centralized domain-level analytics

Consolidating these technical silos into governed domains, the DCRP reduces infrastructure complexity and allows the integration team to focus on business value rather than manual proxy management.

Figure 8 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP

3 - The API Proxy Design Pattern provides:

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) utilizes the native Proxy Endpoint and Target Endpoint architecture of SAP API Management to decouple business intent from technical execution.

Core Architectural Roles:
  • Proxy Endpoint (The Intelligent Router): Manages high-level business logic, including authentication, rate limiting, and security. It determines "who" is calling and "where" the request should be directed.
  • Target Endpoint (The Dynamic Connector): Functions as the technical bridge that resolves backend destinations at runtime using metadata. It handles the specific connectivity requirements for each backend system.
  • Gateway Layer Enforcement: Centralized policies—including quotas, Spike Arrest throttling, custom JavaScript logic, and analytics collection—are enforced at this layer.

 Figure 9 - API Proxy Pattern Architecture: Request/Response Flow through Gateway Layer

4 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy

This section defines the configuration for the ProxyEndpoint, Routing Rules, and Target Endpoint properties required to deploy a domain-centric architecture.

4.1 - The Entry Contract: One API Proxy per Business Domain

DCRP eliminates proxy sprawl by consolidating multiple technical applications into a single entry contract based on the business domain. This ensures each business area, such as Sales or Finance, has exactly one governed API Proxy.

4.2 - Naming Convention Strategy

A standardized naming convention provides immediate architectural context. DCRP uses the following structure:

[Provider]-[Pattern]-[Domain]-[Sub-Domain]-[Protocol]-api

  • Example: cpi-dcrp-sales-orders-odata-api

    • cpi: Identifies the provider (SAP Cloud Integration)
    • dcrp: Defines the routing methodology.
    • sales-orders: Identifies the business domain/process (O2C).
    • odata: Represents the primary contract protocol.

4.3 - API Proxy Details

The proxy serves as the unified entry point for the domain:

  • Name: cpi-dcrp-sales-orders-odata-api
  • Type: REST
  • Base URL: https://[tenant].apimanagement.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/sales

4.4 - Endpoint & Routing Configuration

The Routing Rules act as a gatekeeper, ensuring only authorized paths reach the dynamic engine.

Routing Rule (Proxy Endpoint):

  • Condition: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"

Target Endpoint Properties:

These properties provide the base connectivity for the routing engine:

PropertyValue
cpi.hosthttps://[tenant].cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com
cpi.basepath/http/orders 
backend.systemsap-cpi

 5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions

With the Proxy and Target configurations established, the focus shifts to the API Contract. DCRP creates a Semantic Facade, offering a clean, unified interface to external consumers while managing technical complexity internally.

Instead of deploying separate proxies for every integration, all consumers interact with the same API Proxy. Differentiation and routing are handled dynamically via path suffixes, custom headers, or correlation IDs.

5.1 - Key Benefits:

  • Total Abstraction: The entry contract for the consumer remains unchanged even if you migrate an iFlow or transition to a native S/4HANA OData service in the future.
  • End-to-End Monitoring: Consolidation into a single proxy allows for unified analytics through the Statistics Collector, providing a comprehensive view of the entire Sales domain in one place.
  • Centralized Security Policies: You can enforce a single security policy (OAuth2, API Key, or Basic Auth) across the domain.
  • Unified Traffic Control: Apply consistent Spike Arrest, Quotas, and payload size restrictions (XML/JSON) for the entire domain based on headers like X-SenderId or X-CorrelationId.

Figure 10 - Semantic Facade

Note: This article focuses on routing logic and analytics. For comprehensive coverage of security policies (OAuth2, API Keys, Spike Arrest, Quotas, Threat Protection, SQL Injection prevention), refer to SAP Community Network security best practices documentation.

6 - The Semantic Bridge: Public API to Technical Endpoints

The Semantic Bridge illustrates how DCRP abstracts technical complexity by providing a unified, clean interface for consumers. At runtime, the Gateway dynamically translates these semantic paths into specific backend iFlows.

6.1 - Key Observations:

  • Single Entry Point: All consumers utilize the same base URL (e.g., https://<apim>/sales/orders/*).
  • Path-Based Routing: The specific target CPI iFlow is determined by the path suffix (e.g., /inbound, /tracking).
  • Consumer Abstraction: External systems remain unaware of the technical CPI endpoints.
  • Backend Flexibility: CPI URLs can be modified without impacting the existing consumer contracts.

6.2 – GET Request Examples: Maintaining Protocol Integrity

DCRP ensures that HTTP protocol integrity is maintained by preserving path parameters and query strings as Camel Headers within SAP CPI.

  • Public API Call: GET https://apim/sales/orders/tracking/INV-2024-001?include=shipments
  • Technical Target CPI URL: https://cpi/http/orders/tracking/INV-2024-001?include=shipments
  • Result in CPI: CamelHttpPath = /INV-2024-001 and CamelHttpQuery = include=shipments

Figure 11 - Semantic Bridge

7 - The Routing Engine: Understanding the API-Proxy Execution Flow

Before analyzing the JavaScript "brain," it is essential to understand the internal execution sequence of the SAP API Management Proxy Design Pattern. The DCRP routing occurs as part of a precise three-stage sequence.

7.1 - The Three-Stage Execution Flow

Every request entering an API Proxy passes through three critical stages:

  • 7.1.1 - Stage 1: Proxy Endpoint Pre-Flow: This is the initial arrival point where the gateway executes security policies (OAuth2, API Key, Basic Auth), request validation (payload size, content type), and traffic control (Spike Arrest, Quotas). Immediate rejection occurs if any policy fails here.
  • 7.1.2 - Stage 2: Routing Rules: After successful pre-flow policies, the gateway evaluates Routing Rules. For example, a condition like proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**" acts as a gatekeeper, forwarding only matching requests to the configured Target Endpoint while rejecting others with a 404 error.
  • 7.1.3 - Stage 3: Target Endpoint Pre-Flow: At this stage, the request has been authenticated, routing rules have been validated, and the gateway is ready to connect to the backend.
7.2 - Why This Sequence Matters for DCRP

The sequence is critical because if any initial policy fails, the request never reaches the DCRP mechanism. Once policies pass and the path condition matches, the message flows to the Target Endpoint Pre-Flow. This is the last point where target.url can be modified before connecting to the backend, which is why all DCRP routing logic resides here.

7.3 - The DCRP Brain: Policy Stages

The routing engine executes through four policy stages:

  • Key/Variable Extraction: Retrieves httppath, iflowname, and sapprocess.
  • JavaScript Routing: Performs variable verification, validation, and injection of headers.
  • RaiseFaultException: Blocks the process in case of exceptions, missing variables, or KVM misconfigurations.
  • Statistics Collection: Captures custom dimension variables for the SAP APIM Dashboard.

Critical Point: The Target Pre-Flow is the LAST point where we can modify target.url before SAP APIM connects to the backend. This is why all DCRP routing logic resides here. The diagram below demonstrates this complete three-stage execution flow.

7.3 - The DCRP Brain executes 4 policy stages:

  • Key/Variable extraction (httppath,iflowname,sapprocess)
  • JavaScript Routing (verification variables, validation, injection of variables and headers)
  • RaiseFaultException ( in case of any exception, miss variables, wrong configuration of VM the javascript blocks de process)
  • Statistics collection (custom dimensions variables for SAP APIM Dashboard)

8 - The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic

The DCRP pattern transforms the gateway into a high-scale, programmable router by decoupling configuration from execution and moving routing logic into metadata.

Figure 12 - KVM + Java Scrpit

8.1 - Key-Value Map (KVM): The Memory

Instead of hardcoding backend destinations, DCRP utilizes Key Value Maps (KVM) as a dynamic routing table. This approach enables near-instant updates without requiring proxy redeployments.

KVM Structure Example:

  • Map Identifier: cpipackage-nx.sales.domain.process
  • Key: iflowname
  • Value: inbound:SO.01.Iflow.Sales.Order.Inbound, tracking:SO.02.Iflow.Sales.Order.Tracking.Provider

Governance Note: Strict adherence to naming conventions and delimiters (commas are recommended) is mandatory to ensure deterministic path resolution by the routing engine.

Figure 13 - KVM Configuration

8.2 Scaling Model: Vertical vs. Horizontal

  • Vertical Scaling (30 Seconds): Add new iFlows to an existing domain by updating the KVM string. No infrastructure changes are required.

  • Horizontal Scaling (2 Minutes): Onboard an entirely new business domain (e.g., Finance) by cloning the domain template and updating only the mapIdentifier.

Figure 13 - Two-Level scaling strategy

8.2.1 - Real-World Validation: 4 Business Domains Deployed

The methodology was validated by deploying four business domains—Sales, Logistics, Finance, and Procurement—managing 8 total iFlows.

Validation Metrics:

  • API Proxies Deployed: 4.
  • JavaScript Policies Reused: 1 universal routing engine.
  • Total Deployment Time: Approximately 8 minutes (~2 minutes per domain).
  • Resilience: While DCRP returns an HTTP 404 if a specific iFlow endpoint is missing, the routing logic itself remains correct and operational.

Total Time = (N domains × 2 min) + (M iFlows × 30 sec)

Parallel Workflow:

  • Day 1: Deploy DCRP proxy (2 min) + stub iFlow → routing validated ✅
  • Day 2-3: Integration team develops full iFlow logic → end-to-end functional ✅

Without iFlow endpoint: DCRP returns HTTP 404, but routing logic is still correct.

8.2.3 - Scalability Constraints (Transparency)

Scalability at the enterprise level is influenced by factors across multiple architectural layers, rather than the DCRP logic in isolation. Maintaining high-scale performance requires addressing constraints in both the management and execution layers.

Key Constraints by Layer:

  • APIM Layer: Scalability is impacted by JavaScript execution overhead and the performance of KVM lookups when dealing with large entry sets. Concurrent request handling capacity is also limited by the specific tenant configuration.
  • CPI Layer: Performance depends on worker node capacity, memory allocation per integration flow, and concurrent iFlow execution limits. Message throughput is also constrained by tenant size (MB/hour).

The ultimate scalability ceiling is a combined result of APIM routing capacity and the backend CPI infrastructure. Therefore, proper sizing of both layers is essential for successful high-scale deployments.

Bottom Line: After testing various approaches—including hardcoding, Target Endpoint properties, and dynamic property generation—KVM emerged as the only method capable of maintaining the resilience and scalability required at an enterprise scale. It is an architectural necessity for the reliability of the DCRP framework.

Figure 14 - Governance is mandatory

8.6 - The Template Philosophy: Scaling Strategies

Scaling DCRP effectively requires choosing between two distinct deployment approaches, depending on the complexity and regional requirements of the enterprise.

Two Strategic Approaches:

  • Approach 1: Single Global Template

    • Description: A single master template is utilized for all business domains.
    • Time per Deployment: Approximately 2 minutes.
    • Configuration: Requires 5 manual updates, including the KVM Policy mapIdentifier, Routing Rules, Target Endpoint cpi.basepath, API Base Path, and optional security policies.
    • Best For: Environments with few domains and low cloning frequency.

  • Approach 2: Domain-Specific Templates (RECOMMENDED)

    • Description: A dedicated template exists for each business domain (e.g., Sales, Finance).

    • Time per Deployment: Approximately 30 seconds.
    • Configuration: Requires only 1 manual update: the API Base Path.
    • Pre-configured Elements: KVM mapIdentifier, Routing Rules, Target Endpoint cpi.basepath, and domain-appropriate security policies are already set.
    • Best For: Enterprise environments with regional or multi-instance deployments.

8.7 - The ROI of Domain Templates

As the number of regional instances increases, the efficiency of Domain-Specific Templates becomes superior:

Regional InstancesGlobal TemplateDomain TemplateSavings
10 instances20 min14.5 min5.5 min (27%)
20 instances40 min19.5 min20.5 min (51%)
50 instances100 min34.5 min65.5 min (65%)

  • Break-even Point: 5 instances per domain.

  • ROI: After 5 clones, every additional deployment is 4× faster.

Figure 15 - Global Template vs Domain Template

Real-World Lesson Learned: The "Ghost KVM" 404

During a recent validation, deploying Finance and Procurement proxies resulted in immediate 404 errors.

  • Root Cause: A manual configuration error where a proxy cloned from the Sales domain still pointed to the Sales KVM mapIdentifier instead of its own.
  • Impact: The JavaScript engine searched the Sales KVM for Finance-specific paths (e.g., /finance/invoices), found no match, and triggered a 404.
  • The Solution: Correcting the mapIdentifier took 2 minutes.
  • Prevention: By utilizing Domain Templates, this error is eliminated because the mapIdentifier is pre-configured correctly within the template.

8.8 - The Domain Template Contract: Efficiency Through Pre-Configuration

The Domain Template approach is designed to eliminate human error by locking in the complex configurations once, allowing for rapid and safe replication across instances.

What’s Pre-Configured (Zero Risk of Forgetting):

  • Proxy Endpoint - PreFlow:

    • Authentication: Domain-specific security like Basic Auth for Sales or OAuth2 for Finance.
    • Threat Protection: Policies tailored to the specific domain's requirements.
    • Quota & Spike Arrest: Pre-set thresholds to prevent traffic surges.

  • Target Endpoint - PreFlow:

    • KVM Lookup: The mapIdentifier is already correctly pointed to the domain metadata.
    • JavaScript "The Brain": The universal routing logic is already embedded.
    • RaiseFault & Statistics Collector: Universal error handling and analytics are ready to go.
    • cpi.basepath: Already set correctly for the target domain.

What You Change (30 seconds):

  • API Base Path: Simply update the identifier (e.g., /sales → /sales-eu).
  • Deploy: The configuration is complete.

9 - Deployment Checklist (Domain Template Strategy)

To achieve maximum velocity, the process is split into a one-time setup and a repeatable deployment.

One-Time Setup (Per Domain - ~10 min):

  1. Clone the Global Template to create a specific Domain Template.
  2. Configure domain-specific security, KVM mapIdentifier, and cpi.basepath.
  3. Set the Routing Rule for that domain.
  4. Save as Template (do NOT deploy).

Repeatable Deployment (Per Instance - ~30 sec):

  1. Clone the Domain Template.
  2. Change the API Base Path.
  3. Deploy and Test.

9.1 - Critical Pre-Requisite for DCRP Validation

The 30-second deployment time assumes the iFlow endpoint is already deployed and available on CPI.

  • Practice over Perfection: The iFlow does not need to be fully developed; a minimal "Hello World" or stub endpoint is sufficient to validate routing.
  • Parallel Development: This allows the API Team to deploy the DCRP proxy in 30 seconds while the Integration Team develops full logic in parallel.

Parallel Development Workflow

DayAPI Team ActivityIntegration Team ActivityStatus
Day 1Deploy Finance proxy (30 sec)Create stub iFlow endpointRouting Validated
Day 2Deploy Sales proxy (30 sec)Add data mapping logicRouting Still Works
Day 3Deploy Logistics proxy (30 sec)Integrate backend systemsEnd-to-End Functional
 
Without iFlow endpoint available: DCRP proxy will return HTTP 404 or connection timeout — but the APIM routing itself is still correct.

Bottom line:

  • DCRP deployment time = 30 seconds
  • iFlow endpoint availability = prerequisite (can be stub)
  • Full iFlow development = parallel track (does not block APIM deployment)

Figure 16 -Template process deploy

9. The Brain: Dissecting the JavaScript Routing Engine

The JavaScript engine is the core of the DCRP governance methodology, transforming incoming requests into governed, routed transactions through a precise 6-stage execution flow.

9.1 Execution Flow:
  • Context Extraction: Retrieves pathsuffix, X-SenderId, and X-CorrelationId from the runtime environment.
  • Flexible Delimiter Detection: Automatically detects KVM separators (,, ;, |), supporting diverse formatting without breaking logic.
  • Longest-Path Matching: Sorts routes by length to ensure a request for /orders/tracking hits the specific tracking iFlow rather than a generic /orders handler.
  • Governance Header Injection: Injects SenderId and CorrelationId for SAP ALM traceability and cross-system log correlation.
  • Dynamic URL Construction: Constructs the final destination using the logic: target.url = host + basepath + matched_iflow + query_parameters.
  • Auto-Copy Override: Sets target.copy.pathsuffix to false, preventing double-appending segments—the leading cause of 404 errors in dynamic routing.
9.2 Resilience & Exception Handling

The "Brain" follows a Fail-Fast philosophy. If a KVM lookup fails or mandatory headers are missing, the script triggers a routing.failed flag. This invokes a Raise Fault policy returning a structured error (e.g., DCRP_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND), ensuring failures are transparent rather than "black boxes".

9.3- The Complete JavaScript Code
Spoiler
/**
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 * DCRP - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern
 * The Commander's Brain (Viana Pattern)
 * Version: 1.3 - Production Ready with Flexible Delimiters
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 * 
 * CAPABILITIES:
 * - Dynamic iFlow resolution via metadata (KVM)
 * - Path parameter preservation
 * - Query string integrity across gateways
 * - Governance header injection (X-SenderId, X-CorrelationId)
 * - Analytics variable collection
 * - Fault triggers for error scenarios
 * - Zero-code deployment for new integrations (metadata-only)
 * 
 * Author: Viana, SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
 * Pattern: DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern)
 * ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 */

function resolveDcprRouting() {
    
    // SECTION 1: CONTEXT EXTRACTION
    var targetBase = context.getVariable("target.cpi.host");
    var cpiBasePath = context.getVariable("target.cpi.basepath");
    var kvmMapIflowString = context.getVariable("iflowname");
    var rawSuffix = context.getVariable("proxy.pathsuffix") || "";
    
    // Governance headers
    var senderId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-SenderId") || "UnknownSender";
    var corId = context.getVariable("request.header.X-CorrelationId") || "NoCorrID";
    
    var iflowName = "UnknownIflow";
    var resourceName = "";
    var matchedKey = "";

    // SECTION 2: FLEXIBLE DELIMITER DETECTION & KVM PARSING
    // Supported delimiters (colon reserved for path:iflow separation)
    var allowedDelimiters = [',', '/', '\\', '[', ']', '(', ')', '-', ';', '|', '_'];
    
    var detectedDelimiter = ','; // default
    
    // Detect which delimiter is used in the KVM string
    if (kvmMapIflowString) {
        for (var d = 0; d < allowedDelimiters.length; d++) {
            if (kvmMapIflowString.indexOf(allowedDelimiters[d]) !== -1) {
                detectedDelimiter = allowedDelimiters[d];
                break;
            }
        }
        
        // Split by detected delimiter and parse routes
        var routes = kvmMapIflowString.split(detectedDelimiter).map(function(entry) {
            return entry.trim();
        });
        
        // Sort routes by pathKey length (longest first for specificity)
        var parsedRoutes = [];
        for (var i = 0; i < routes.length; i++) {
            if (routes[i] === "") continue;
            
            var splitResult = routes[i].split(':').map(function(part) {
                return part.trim();
            });
            
            if (splitResult.length >= 2) {
                parsedRoutes.push({
                    pathKey: splitResult[0],
                    iflowName: splitResult[1]
                });
            }
        }
        
        // Sort by length (longest pathKey first to match most specific routes)
        parsedRoutes.sort(function(a, b) {
            return b.pathKey.length - a.pathKey.length;
        });
        
        // Find matching route
        for (var r = 0; r < parsedRoutes.length; r++) {
            var pathKey = parsedRoutes[r].pathKey;
            if (rawSuffix.indexOf("/" + pathKey) !== -1) {
                matchedKey = pathKey;
                resourceName = "/" + pathKey;
                iflowName = parsedRoutes[r].iflowName;
                break;
            }
        }
    }

    // SECTION 3: VALIDATION & FAULT DETECTION
    if (!targetBase || targetBase === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_CONFIG_ERROR: target.cpi.host is not configured");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!cpiBasePath || cpiBasePath === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_CONFIG_ERROR: target.cpi.basepath is not configured");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!senderId || senderId === "UnknownSender") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_GOVERNANCE_ERROR: X-SenderId header is missing or empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!corId || corId === "NoCorrID") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_GOVERNANCE_ERROR: X-CorrelationId header is missing or empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (!kvmMapIflowString || kvmMapIflowString === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_KVM_ERROR: iflowname KVM entry is empty");
        return;
    }
    
    if (iflowName === "UnknownIflow" || resourceName === "") {
        context.setVariable("routing.failed", "true");
        context.setVariable("routing.error.cause", "DCRP_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND: No iFlow mapping found for path: " + rawSuffix);
        return;
    }

    // SECTION 4: GOVERNANCE HEADER INJECTION
    context.targetRequest.headers['X-SenderId'] = senderId;
    context.targetRequest.headers['X-CorrelationId'] = corId;

    // SECTION 5: DYNAMIC URL CONSTRUCTION
    var finalUrl = targetBase.toString() + cpiBasePath.toString() + resourceName;
    
    // Extract extra path after matched resource
    if (matchedKey !== "") {
        var pathSegments = rawSuffix.split("/" + matchedKey);
        if (pathSegments.length > 1 && pathSegments[1] !== "" && pathSegments[1] !== "/") {
            var extraPath = pathSegments[1].split("?")[0];
            finalUrl = finalUrl + extraPath;
        }
    }
    
    // Preserve query string
    var queryString = context.getVariable("request.querystring");
    if (queryString) {
        finalUrl = finalUrl + "?" + queryString;
    }

    // SECTION 6: TARGET CONFIGURATION
    context.setVariable("target.copy.pathsuffix", false);
    context.setVariable("target.copy.queryparams", false);
    context.setVariable("target.url", finalUrl);

    // SECTION 7: ANALYTICS VARIABLE INJECTION
    context.setVariable("custom.idsender", senderId);
    context.setVariable("custom.idiflow", iflowName);
    context.setVariable("custom.idcorrelation", corId);
    
    context.setVariable("routing.failed", "false");
}

9.4 - How the Routing Engine Works

The JavaScript engine is the technical "brain" of the DCRP. It executes seven critical operations in a precise sequence to resolve targets dynamically.

Image 17 - How the Routing Engine Works - VM + JSc

Section 1: Context Extraction

The engine begins by retrieving six essential variables from the SAP APIM runtime context to establish the routing environment:

  • target.cpi.host: The base URL of the CPI tenant.
  • target.cpi.basepath: The specific path prefix for CPI (e.g., /http/orders).
  • iflowname: The raw metadata string from the KVM containing the route mappings.
  • proxy.pathsuffix: The segment of the path following the proxy base (e.g., /inbound/12345).
  • X-SenderId: A governance header used for tracking the originating system.
  • X-CorrelationId: A header for end-to-end transaction tracing across systems.

Section 2: Flexible Delimiter Detection and KVM Parsing

To ensure compatibility across different team preferences, the engine automatically detects the delimiter used within the KVM string.

  • Supported Delimiters: Includes comma, forward slash, backslash, brackets, parentheses, hyphen, semicolon, pipe, and underscore.
  • Reserved Character: The colon (:) is strictly reserved for separating the semantic path from the technical iFlow name.

10 - API Contracts, Versioning & Openapi Management

10.1 - Why DCRP Proxies Don't Need Versioning

In traditional API management, versioning is a primary driver of Infrastructure Sprawl. Every new version typically spawns a new proxy (e.g., v1-api, v2-api), forcing architects to manage multiple redundant infrastructures.

DCRP eliminates this proliferation by recognizing that the proxy is a stateless router with an immutable base path, not the owner of the API contract.

  • Fixed Entry Point: The proxy base path remains constant (e.g., /sales/orders/**).
  • Versioned Routing: API versions live in the path suffix (e.g., /v1/, /v2/).
  • Decoupled Logic: These versions are mapped to specific iFlows via KVM metadata, keeping the proxy itself singular and unchanged.

Real-World Example: Adding Version 4

StepTraditional 1:1 Proxy ModelDCRP Model
ActionCreate new proxy, configure security, routing, and targets, then redeploy.Deploy new iFlow and update a single KVM metadata entry.
Time Effort~15 minutes~30 seconds
OutcomeManaging 4 separate proxy infrastructures.Still managing 1 proxy infrastructure.

Client Interaction Examples:

  • Legacy: POST /sales/orders/v1/inbound → Routes to v1 iFlow.
  • Current: POST /sales/orders/v2/inbound → Routes to v2 iFlow.
  • New: POST /sales/orders/v4/inbound → Routes to v4 iFlow.
  • Latest (Default): POST /sales/orders/inbound → Dynamically routes to v4 via KVM.

The proxy base path /sales/orders/** never changed., providing a stable, governed interface for consumers regardless of backend evolution.Figure 18 - Contracts & Governance

10.2 - OpenAPI Management: The Hybrid Approach

In the DCRP model, API contracts (OpenAPI specifications) are decoupled from both the proxy and the iFlow, residing in a central API Portal or repository. This hybrid model ensures a clean separation of concerns:

  • KVM: Stores runtime routing metadata (path → endpoint).

  • OpenAPI: Stores design-time contract metadata (request/response schemas).

  • API Portal: Provides discovery, documentation, and governance.

The Execution Flow:

When a developer needs to integrate, they download the spec (e.g., sales-orders-v2.yaml) from the Portal and generate their client code. When they call the DCRP proxy at /sales/orders/v2, the proxy consults the KVM and routes the request to the corresponding v2 iFlow.

Figure 20- Hybrid Contracts

10.3 - KVM vs OpenAPI: Complementary, Not Redundant

At first glance, KVM and OpenAPI might seem to overlap. Both describe APIs. But they serve distinct purposes:

KVM answers: "Where should I route this request?"

  • Input: /sales/orders/v2
  • Output: https://cpi-tenant.cfapps.../http/orders/inbound

OpenAPI answers: "What schema should this request/response have?"

  • Input: POST /sales/orders/v2
  • Output: Request body must include {orderId, customerId, items[]}, returns {status, orderNumber}

Both are necessary for complete governance:

  • Without KVM: No dynamic routing (back to static proxy-per-integration)
  • Without OpenAPI: No contract validation (clients and servers drift out of sync)

DCRP uses KVM for runtime routing decisions and OpenAPI for design-time contract definition and optional runtime validation.

Figure 21 - Complementary - DCRP and OpenAPI

10.4 - Version Coexistence and Deprecation

Multiple API versions coexist seamlessly within the KVM. For example, /v1 can point to a maintenance iFlow while /v2 and the default root path /sales/orders point to the latest stable version.

Deprecation Strategy:

  • Monitor: Use analytics to track legacy version usage.
  • Remove: Once usage drops, simply delete the KVM entry for that version.
  • Result: Clients calling the old version receive a clear 404 Not Found with a routing.failed error.

Final Thought: By omitting the version suffix, clients can opt into the default path to always receive the latest stable version automatically.

11 -Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence

While the JavaScript "Brain" handles routing, the Statistics Collector provides the visibility necessary for production management. This policy captures technical metadata and transforms it into actionable business intelligence within the SAP API Management analytics database.

  • Purpose: Records routing metadata such as iFlow name, package name, domain process, and tracking IDs.
  • Utility: Enables real-time monitoring, trend analysis, and data-driven capacity planning.

Policy Configuration Snippet:

 

<!--Specify Stats collector policy to add custom dimensions and measures -->
<StatisticsCollector async='false' continueOnError='false' enabled='true'
	xmlns='http://www.sap.com/apimgmt'>
	<Statistics>
		<!-- prepopulated template -->
		<!-- Uncomment Statistic which needs to be collected and define reference variable-->
		<Statistic name='custom_idcorrelation' ref='idcorrelation' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idflow' ref='idiflow' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idpackage' ref='idpackage' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsaprocess' ref='idsapprocess' type='string'>0</Statistic>
		<Statistic name='custom_idsender' ref='idsender' type='string'>0</Statistic>
	</Statistics>
</StatisticsCollector>

 

Before you think about scaling this proposal I'm offering, pause and prepare. This is not a "quick win" implementation. You need to:

  • Align your team – Developers, architects, and platform owners must understand the paradigm shift
  • Educate stakeholders – Business sponsors need to grasp why consolidation beats proliferation
  • Establish governance – Define clear ownership, approval workflows, and change management processes
  • Design naming conventions – The [provider]-[pattern]-[domain]-[subdomain]-[protocol]-api structure is non-negotiable
  • Structure a PoC first – Validate with 2-3 iFlows in a sandbox before scaling to production

12. Final Roadmap: Preparing for Scale

Before scaling this architecture, a "pause and prepare" phase is essential to ensure long-term sustainability. This is a fundamental paradigm shift, not a simple technical patch.

Pre-Scaling Checklist:

Team Alignment: Ensure developers, architects, and platform owners understand the move from 1:1 proxies to domain-centric routing.

  • Stakeholder Education: Help business sponsors understand how consolidation reduces infrastructure costs and complexity.
  • Establish Governance: Define clear ownership and approval workflows for KVM metadata updates.
  • Enforce Standards: Strict adherence to the [provider]-[pattern]-[domain]-[subdomain]-[protocol]-api naming convention is mandatory.
  • Sandbox PoC: Validate the logic with 2–3 iFlows in a non-production environment before a full rollout.

Conclusion: Discipline Over Chaos

As an SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert, I have witnessed the struggle of managing sprawling landscapes. The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) provides the governance layer that SAP API Management needs but does not offer out-of-the-box.

Benefits of the DCRP

  • Removes Complexity: It shifts the technical OData burden away from the consumer.
  • Maintains Semantic Integrity: It cleanly separates the Business Domain (API Management) from the Technical Orchestration (CPI).
  • Future Proof: Consumer contracts remain unchanged regardless of backend service migrations or version updates.

Figure 26 - The Commander: Discipline Over Chaos

Implementing DCRP, you transform your gateway into a scalable, semantic, and governed ecosystem rather than a disorganized collection of endpoints.

The Roadmap Ahead

This is only the first step in mastering enterprise-scale integration:

  • Part I: DCRP – Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (Gateway Layer) 📍- YOU ARE HERE - 
  • Part II: PDCP – Package-Domain-Centric Pattern (Orchestration Layer): Solving CPI Package and User Sprawl ⏱️[COMING SOON].
  • Part III: Testing & Monitoring – DCRP + PDCP Validation with SAP ALM ⏱️[COMING SOON].
  • Part IV: Governance & Operations – Organizational Sustainability ⏱️ [TBD].

"In architecture, as in the field, discipline is not about restriction; it is about creating the boundaries that allow your forces to scale without chaos." — The Commander, Ricardo Viana

If this article helped you, please like, share, and reach out if you are implementing DCRP in your landscape!

Kind Regards,

Viana | SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
